result:
:::writing
The first thing the city forgot was its shadows.

No one noticed at first. Why would they? Shadows are a kind of background promise, like gravity or the hum behind the refrigerator at night. You don’t check for them. You assume they’re there because they’ve always been there, obediently stretching and shrinking, clinging to your heels like loyal animals.

Then one morning, a man crossing 8th Street stopped mid-stride and turned around, certain someone had called his name. No one had. What startled him wasn’t a voice—it was the clean, unbroken pavement behind him. No echo of his shape. No darker twin stitched to his feet.

He looked down at himself, as if his shadow might have slipped under his coat.

It hadn’t.

By noon, the city was full of people quietly looking down.

At first, they blamed the light. Something about the angle, someone said. Atmospheric conditions. A temporary distortion. Phones came out. Photos were taken. Filters were blamed. But even in the photographs—no shadows. The people in the images looked cut out and pasted onto the world, like they didn’t quite belong to the ground beneath them.

By evening, the city had a new kind of silence. Not the absence of noise, but the absence of certainty.

—

Mara noticed because of her cat.

The cat, a gray creature named Lantern, had always been obsessed with shadows. He chased them, stalked them, pounced on them with the solemn intensity of a creature who believed this time—this time—he would catch one.

That evening, Lantern sat in the middle of the living room floor and did nothing.

No pouncing. No twitching. Just a stillness so complete it seemed unnatural.

Mara flicked the lamp on and off. The room brightened and dimmed. The furniture shifted tone. But nothing cast.

“Lantern,” she said, softly, as if not to alarm the absence.

The cat looked up at her, and for a strange, fleeting second, Mara had the uncomfortable sense that he was disappointed in her. As if she had broken something.

—

The news took a full day to catch up.

Experts appeared. Words like “optical anomaly,” “collective perceptual shift,” and “localized dimensional variance” floated across screens with the confidence of people who hoped language could still control reality.

A physicist with tired eyes admitted, “We don’t have a model for this.”

That was the moment people began to panic.

Because as long as someone had a model, even a fragile one, the world still felt held together. But without one, everything felt negotiable.

—

On the third day, people began reporting something stranger.

They weren’t just missing shadows.

They were missing pieces.

A woman couldn’t remember the face of her brother, though she could describe his height, his voice, the way he stirred his coffee counterclockwise. A taxi driver forgot the route he had driven for twenty years, though his hands still turned the wheel at the right moments, guided by something older than memory.

Mara noticed it in small ways.

She reached for a word—something simple, like “window”—and found herself pausing, circling it, approaching it from different angles before finally landing on it like a bird unsure of its footing.

Lantern, too, had changed.

He no longer chased anything. He slept more, but it wasn’t restful. His body twitched, paws flicking as if running through dreams that had lost their landscapes.

At night, Mara began to feel something she couldn’t name: a sense that she was slightly misaligned with herself, like a painting hung just a few degrees crooked.

—

On the fifth day, the first shadow returned.

It happened in a subway station.

A boy standing too close to the platform edge saw it first—not attached to himself, but lying across the tracks like a spill of ink. It moved independently, rippling, as if stirred by a wind no one else could feel.

People gathered.

The shadow stretched, thinned, and then—slowly—rose.

Not up from the ground, but out of it.

It took shape the way smoke does: uncertain, shifting, suggestive rather than precise. And then, with a strange, terrible gentleness, it leaned toward the nearest person.

A woman in a red coat.

The shadow hovered at her feet, hesitating.

Then it attached.

Her body jolted, not violently, but with a subtle correction, like something clicking into place. She gasped—not in fear, but in recognition.

“I remember,” she whispered.

“What?” someone asked.

She blinked, tears suddenly spilling over.

“Everything I forgot,” she said.

—

The city changed again after that.

People began searching for shadows.

Not their own, at first—any shadow. Reports spread of them appearing in alleyways, under bridges, in the quiet corners of parks. Always alone at first. Always hesitant.

And always, when they attached, something returned.

A memory. A feeling. A piece of identity that had slipped loose.

But not always the right piece.

A man emerged from an encounter convinced he had lived an entirely different life—a baker in a coastal town he had never visited. A teenager began speaking a language no one around her understood, though she insisted it was hers.

The shadows were not labeled.

They did not come with instructions.

—

Mara resisted.

For days, she avoided the places where shadows had been reported. She kept to well-lit streets, to open spaces, to the safety of absence.

But the gaps in her mind were growing.

She forgot the melody of a song she used to love. She forgot why she had kept a particular photograph on her desk. She forgot, once, in a moment that left her breathless with fear, where she was going while she was already on her way there.

Lantern stopped eating.

That was what broke her.

She found him one morning staring at the wall, not at anything—just at the idea of something. His body was there, but his attention had thinned, stretched, like butter scraped over too much bread.

“Okay,” she said, her voice steadier than she felt. “Okay.”

—

She found the shadow in an underpass.

It lay along the curved wall, elongated by a light source that no longer seemed to matter. It looked almost ordinary—like the kind of shadow you might ignore a hundred times a day.

But it wasn’t attached to anything.

It was waiting.

Mara stood a few feet away, her heart beating in a way that felt both urgent and distant, as if it belonged to someone else.

“How do I know you’re mine?” she asked.

The shadow did not respond.

Of course it didn’t.

She took a step closer.

Lantern, cradled in her arms, lifted his head slightly, his eyes tracking the shape with a flicker of something—recognition, or longing, or fear.

“Or if that matters,” she added quietly.

Because that was the real question, wasn’t it?

Not whether the shadow belonged to her.

But whether she would still belong to herself after.

She knelt.

The shadow stretched toward her, subtle but undeniable, like a tide responding to a distant moon.

For a moment, she hesitated—not out of fear, but out of a strange, sudden grief for the version of herself that had existed without knowing this choice was possible.

Then she let it touch her.

The world did not shatter.

There was no flash of light, no dramatic shift.

Just a quiet, internal rearrangement.

Memories rushed in—not all at once, but in layers. A childhood afternoon she had forgotten. The exact shade of her mother’s laugh. The reason she had named the cat Lantern: because he had once, as a kitten, followed her through a blackout, his small body a moving certainty in the dark.

She gasped, tears coming before she could stop them.

Lantern stirred in her arms, then wriggled free, landing softly on the ground.

For the first time in days, he pounced.

Not at the shadow.

At her.

Climbing her leg, demanding attention, alive again in a way that was immediate and undeniable.

Mara laughed—a sound that felt like it had been waiting.

But as she stood, steadying herself, she noticed something that made her breath catch.

Her shadow was back.

But it didn’t quite match.

When she lifted her hand, it followed—but a fraction of a second too late.

When she turned, it turned—but not in perfect sync.

It was hers.

And it wasn’t.

Mara watched it for a long moment, then exhaled slowly.

“Okay,” she said again, softer this time.

The city around her was full of people relearning themselves, piece by mismatched piece. Full of shadows that had returned, but not always to where they began.

It wasn’t what had been lost.

It wasn’t what had been.

But it was something.

And for now, something was enough.

Her shadow shifted at her feet, just slightly out of time, like a companion learning her rhythm.

Or teaching her a new one.

Mara turned toward home.

This time, she knew exactly where she was going.

Even if the path felt different beneath her feet.
:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
